applications.title: Build a Tiny String Calculator Kata
applications.summary: Turn red-green-refactor into a concrete habit by shipping a tiny calculator that grows through deliberately small tests.
applications.prompt: Create a small Python package called `string_calculator` that starts with one passing case, then grows through comma-separated inputs, newlines, and invalid-input behavior one failing test at a time.
applications.deliverable: A runnable Python project with a focused README, a passing test suite, and a short log explaining each red-green-refactor step you took.
steps: Start with one failing test for a single number input.
Grow the parser one behavior at a time instead of designing the whole interface upfront.
After each green test, note whether refactoring improved the next likely step.
Write a brief README section describing how the design emerged from the test sequence.
success_criteria: The project history or notes show multiple small TDD cycles rather than one large implementation drop.
The README explains at least one refactor that made the next test easier to express.
The suite stays readable and fast enough to rerun continuously.
extension_ideas: Add custom delimiter support without rewriting earlier tests.
Record a short screencast narrating your red-green-refactor decisions.

applications.title: Harden a File-Backed Test Harness
applications.summary: Practice isolation by building a tiny app that writes files and proving your tests leave no residue behind.
applications.prompt: Build a command-line note saver that writes and reads JSON notes from disk, then design its tests so every case controls setup and teardown explicitly.
applications.deliverable: A small CLI project plus a test harness that uses temp directories or fixtures to guarantee clean state across runs.
steps: Write one end-to-end test that saves and reloads a note using an isolated temp path.
Add failure-path tests for malformed files or missing storage.
Make cleanup deterministic so rerunning the suite in any order still passes.
Document which parts you kept realistic and which parts you isolated.
success_criteria: Tests never depend on manually cleaned folders or pre-existing files.
Async or persistence boundaries are handled intentionally rather than implicitly.
The suite can run repeatedly without flaky state leakage.
extension_ideas: Swap disk storage for SQLite behind a seam and keep the tests trustworthy.
Add an async save path and document how you controlled event-loop scope.

applications.title: Migrate a Legacy unittest Slice to pytest
applications.summary: Use the framework tradeoffs in a practical migration rather than arguing about them abstractly.
applications.prompt: Take a small `unittest`-style module or a toy legacy test file and migrate only the parts where pytest materially improves readability, fixtures, or parametrization.
applications.deliverable: A before-and-after repository slice showing what stayed in unittest, what moved to pytest, and why.
steps: Run the legacy tests under pytest first without rewriting them.
Identify one repeated pattern where fixtures or parametrization reduce friction.
Migrate only that slice and compare clarity, not just syntax.
Write a migration note explaining what remained in place and why.
success_criteria: The migration is pressure-driven rather than total-rewrite theater.
You can point to one concrete ergonomics gain from the pytest-native slice.
Compatibility constraints are documented honestly.
extension_ideas: Add a parametrized edge-case matrix for one behavior.
Turn your migration note into an internal engineering guideline.

applications.title: Wrap a Third-Party API Behind an Owned Seam
applications.summary: Design a small project around an adapter so your tests target behavior instead of vendor call shapes.
applications.prompt: Build a tiny weather, email, or currency-conversion service wrapper with an owned interface and tests that exercise your business logic without coupling directly to the external client.
applications.deliverable: A small service module with an adapter, business logic tests, and a note showing what contract the seam protects.
steps: Define the business-facing interface before choosing how the external call is made.
Write tests against the owned seam rather than the vendor client API.
Keep one realistic boundary test so you do not lose external behavior entirely.
Document how the seam would let you replace the provider later.
success_criteria: Business rules are testable without importing third-party response shapes everywhere.
At least one realistic boundary check remains in the suite.
The seam makes provider replacement or persistence changes cheaper.
extension_ideas: Add retry or fallback behavior behind the same seam.
Introduce a second adapter implementation and keep the calling code unchanged.

applications.title: Replace Brittle Deep Mocks with a Verified Fake
applications.summary: Build a more trustworthy collaborator strategy by comparing a strict mock approach against a fake with real behavior.
applications.prompt: Create a small notification or payment workflow where you first test with strict mocks, then replace the deeper infrastructure simulation with a verified fake and compare the results.
applications.deliverable: A repository slice containing both approaches, plus a short comparison of confidence, brittleness, and maintenance cost.
steps: Start with strict mocks around an owned collaborator.
Notice where the mocks begin to model infrastructure rather than business intent.
Introduce a verified fake or contract-style check for the deeper boundary.
Write up which parts stayed mocked and which parts moved to higher-fidelity verification.
success_criteria: Mocks remain narrow and intentional rather than sprawling across system boundaries.
The verified fake or contract check demonstrates behavior a deep mock could miss.
You can explain the tradeoff without resorting to blanket anti-mock slogans.
extension_ideas: Add autospec and patch-location mistakes intentionally, then document the failure modes.
Turn the comparison into a team decision rubric.

applications.title: Broaden a Real Parser with Parametrized and Property Tests
applications.summary: Apply advanced specification techniques by hardening a small but realistic parser or validator.
applications.prompt: Choose a small parser, formatter, or validator function and expand its coverage with parametrized examples plus at least one property-based invariant.
applications.deliverable: A mini project where one real behavior is covered by both concrete case tables and generated invariants, with notes on what each style revealed.
tools: hypothesis or equivalent property testing tool
steps: Start from a function that already has a few concrete example tests.
Refactor repeated cases into parametrized tests.
Add at least one generated property that expresses an invariant or round-trip behavior.
Record one surprising falsifying example and what it taught you.
success_criteria: Concrete examples and generated tests complement each other rather than duplicate effort.
A property expresses a real invariant, not just random inputs for spectacle.
The test suite still communicates intent clearly.
extension_ideas: Add an async or persistence boundary and decide which broader technique still fits.
Publish the parser as a small utility package.

applications.title: Write a TDD Adoption Plan for a Real Team
applications.summary: Turn the evidence limits into an adoption artifact instead of a generic opinion about TDD.
applications.prompt: Draft a concrete adoption plan for one real team or codebase, covering where TDD is likely to help, where evidence is thin, and what outcomes you would measure locally.
applications.deliverable: A short project brief or internal memo with scope, pilot area, risks, metrics, and rollback criteria.
steps: Choose a real team, repo, or problem area rather than talking in abstractions.
State which TDD outcome claims you are willing to rely on and which you are not.
Define a narrow pilot, success metrics, and conditions that would invalidate the approach.
Name the gaps you would still need to validate inside the target environment.
success_criteria: The plan distinguishes evidence-backed expectations from optimistic assumptions.
Metrics are concrete enough to inspect after a pilot.
The rollout scope is small enough to learn from without organizational theater.
extension_ideas: Convert the memo into a lightweight pilot charter template for future teams.
Pair it with one of the earlier module projects as the pilot workload.
